planting.note: Plant bare-root crowns or runner plants in early spring as soon as soil can be worked; in zones 3–8 plant March–April. Earliglow performs best where reliable cold winters supply the chilling hours needed for strong flowering — in zones 9–10 day-neutral varieties (Albion, Seascape) are better choices due to lower chilling requirements. Pinch all flowers in year 1 to direct energy into runner and crown development for maximum year 2 harvest. Plant in a matted-row or hill system 12–18 inches apart.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, slightly acidic, fertile soil with excellent moisture retention — strawberries are shallow-rooted and require consistent moisture; Earliglow is widely regarded by strawberry enthusiasts as one of the finest-flavored June-bearing varieties available, producing medium-sized, conical, deep-red berries with intense, aromatic, old-fashioned strawberry flavor uncommon in modern commercial varieties bred for shipping durability rather than taste. Earliglow is the earliest of all June-bearing strawberries — the first variety to ripen each season, typically one to two weeks ahead of mid-season types — and the red color extends through the flesh to the center (no white core), reflecting the full flavor development. The intense, sweet-tart, richly aromatic flavor is most comparable to wild strawberries and old heirloom varieties rather than the large but bland commercial berries in grocery stores. Earliglow produces most reliably in zones 3–8 where winter chilling is adequate; gardeners in the Gulf South should choose day-neutral types instead.
sources: Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Strawberry Variety Trial
University of Maryland Extension — Earliglow June-Bearing Strawberry
USDA Agricultural Research Service — Small Fruit Variety Evaluation

planting.note: Plant rooted cuttings or divisions in spring or fall; in zone 9 plant year-round except in peak summer heat. Kentucky Colonel spreads aggressively by underground runners — plant in a buried container or with root barriers to prevent takeover of surrounding beds. In zone 9 partial shade reduces aggressive spread while maintaining large, high-quality leaves; harvest frequently to prevent flowering, which diminishes leaf flavor. Cut to the ground after flowering and the plant will regenerate with fresh, flavorful new growth.

directions: Plant in partial shade to full sun in moist, well-drained, moderately fertile soil — mint thrives with consistent moisture and morning sun with afternoon shade protection; Kentucky Colonel is widely considered the finest spearmint cultivar for culinary use, producing very large, broad, slightly crinkled, bright green leaves with a smooth, intensely sweet, clean spearmint fragrance and flavor that is more refined and less harsh than standard commercial spearmint. The large leaf size makes Kentucky Colonel practical for fresh cocktail garnishes (mint juleps, mojitos), cold drinks, and herb preparations where leaf presentation matters — the substantial leaves are picked individually for floating in glasses or bruising for infusions, rather than stripped from stems. The Kentucky Colonel name connects this variety to the classic bourbon-and-mint culture of Kentucky where spearmint was historically associated with the mint julep tradition — the sweet spearmint character complements bourbon's caramel and vanilla notes without the sharp mentholated edge of peppermint. Kentucky Colonel is also excellent in iced tea, fruit salads, desserts, and fresh herb sauces where a classic spearmint flavor is desired.
sources: University of Kentucky Cooperative Extension — Culinary Mint Varieties
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Specialty Herb Production for the South
North Carolina State University Extension — Mint Cultivar Selection

planting.note: Direct sow or transplant 3–6 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 sow September–February for fall through spring harvest. Winterbor is an F1 hybrid with exceptional cold hardiness — repeated hard freezes convert the leaf starches to sugars, making late-winter harvested Winterbor noticeably sweeter than fall-harvested leaves. In zone 9 fall planting in September–October produces excellent quality through spring; the plants are one of the most productive over-wintering vegetables for zone 9 kitchen gardens. Harvest outer leaves from the bottom up to keep plants producing.

directions: Plant in full sun in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Winterbor is an F1 hybrid curly kale developed for exceptional vigor, uniformity, and extraordinary cold hardiness — the 'Winter' in the name reflects its ability to withstand severe freezes that damage most other kale varieties, making it the standard commercial and home garden curly kale in northern climates. The very densely curled, heavily blistered, blue-green leaves are the archetypal kale-chip and sautéed-kale leaf — the extreme surface area created by tight curling traps oil and seasonings, making Winterbor the variety most frequently used in kale chip recipes where maximum crispiness requires maximum surface contact with the baking sheet. Unlike flat-leaf or lacinato kale varieties, the heavy curl structure of Winterbor makes the raw leaves quite firm and fibrous — they benefit from massage with oil and salt to soften before use in salads, making it primarily a cooking kale rather than a fresh salad variety. Repeated hard freezes dramatically improve the flavor of all kale, but particularly Winterbor: cold temperatures convert accumulated starches to sugars, making late-winter harvested Winterbor among the sweetest and most palatable versions of this species.
sources: Johnny's Selected Seeds — Winterbor Kale F1 Trials
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Kale Variety Trial
University of Wisconsin Extension — Kale Production in Cold Climates

scientific_name: Brassica rapa subsp. rapa 'Purple Top White Globe'

planting.note: Direct sow 3–6 weeks before last frost for spring harvest; in zone 9 sow September–February for a fall through spring harvest — fall planting produces the finest quality turnips as cool temperatures sweeten the roots and reduce bitterness. Sow thickly and thin to 4–6 inches after seedlings establish; thinnings at 3–4 inches tall are edible as turnip greens. Harvest roots at 2–3 inches diameter for the most tender, mildest flavor — oversize roots become pithy and bitter. Both roots and greens are productive throughout the zone 9 cool season.

directions: Plant in full sun in deep, loose, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil free of stones and compaction; Purple Top White Globe is the definitive American turnip variety — the standard against which all other turnip cultivars are measured, with over a century of continuous cultivation making it the most widely recognized turnip appearance in North America. The smooth, round roots develop their characteristic two-tone coloration from the soil surface: the shoulder (exposed to light above ground) develops a rich, vivid purple-violet pigmentation while the buried portion stays clean creamy white, creating the immediately recognizable bicolor that defines the visual identity of a turnip. Both the roots and the large, hairy, dark green leaves (turnip greens) are edible — the mild, slightly sweet, pleasantly peppery roots can be eaten raw at small size or cooked in soups, stews, and roasted vegetable preparations, while the greens are a classic Southern potherb simmered with smoked pork and eaten alongside collards and mustard. Fall-planted turnips develop the finest flavor in zone 9 as cool temperatures concentrate sugars and reduce any harshness in the roots.
sources: Southern Exposure Seed Exchange — Purple Top White Globe Turnip History
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Turnips for Gulf South Gardens
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Cool-Season Root Vegetables

planting.note: Direct sow 2–4 weeks before last frost; in zone 9 sow September–March for fall through spring harvest. Fordhook Giant is a large, vigorous variety that reaches impressive size in cool conditions — individual leaves can exceed 2 feet long in optimal growing. Harvest outer leaves continuously from the bottom of the plant, leaving the central growing tip intact to keep plants producing; plants can produce for many months with this cut-and-come-again approach. In zone 9 plants are productive from fall through early spring and may bolt in warming spring weather.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil; Fordhook Giant is the classic white-stalked Swiss chard — one of the oldest and most productive chard varieties in American seed catalogs, developed by the Burpee seed company and named for Burpee's Fordhook Farm in Pennsylvania where it was trialed and selected. The very large, heavily blistered, dark green leaves with thick, pure white, celery-like stalks represent the classic Swiss chard appearance and deliver maximum productivity per square foot — the substantial white stalks are among the meatiest and most impressive of any chard variety, making Fordhook Giant the preferred choice in preparations where stalks are eaten separately from the leaves. The thick stalks can be prepared raw like celery, steamed and buttered like asparagus, or braised for their mild, slightly sweet, beet-adjacent flavor; the large leaves cook down like spinach with a mild, earthy, slightly mineral character. Individual plants in good conditions can produce leaves exceeding 2 feet long, making the garden presence of Fordhook Giant among the most dramatically large of any leafy vegetable. The partial shade tolerance makes it one of the best vegetables for kitchen gardens with limited full-sun exposure.
sources: W. Atlee Burpee Company — Fordhook Giant Chard History
Cornell University Cooperative Extension — Swiss Chard Production Guide
University of Florida IFAS Extension — Swiss Chard Varieties for Florida
